speaker: interviewee
text: [BACKGROUND NOISE]

speaker: interviewee
text: He's like--

speaker: interviewer
text: I guess he knows what he can get away with it. [LAUGHS]

speaker: interviewee
text: He-- yeah. And he gets his punishment anyway everyday. And then the baby is so cute. I think he's coming down. Hopefully he's gonna knock when he come. Record to the clown.

speaker: interviewer
text: OK.

speaker: interviewee
text: We have to. [BACKGROUND NOISE]

speaker: interviewer
text: We may-- we may want to leave the door unlocked just so when-- when they come, they don't have to kindly knock.

speaker: interviewee
text: But they-- when--

speaker: interviewer
text: The camera.

speaker: interviewer
text: They're just gonna basically show up on camera and say hello.

speaker: interviewee
text: Yeah, don't write brother.

speaker: interviewer
text: Oh, yeah. Perfect, yeah.

speaker: interviewee
text: If you want, all you have to do is just show up.

speaker: interviewer
text: Right.

speaker: interviewee
text: You don't have-- like, I don't know how.

speaker: interviewer
text: Well, the idea is that after the whole story, you know, it's kind of a more positive note to end on. And it's also like you're passing it on toward future generations when you show the family, and you know, you tape all of those. [BACKGROUND NOISE] Front door, is that OK?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yeah. But when someone may see, they will want to come in. What is-- why has this paper there for?

speaker: interviewer
text: Oh. [LAUGHS]

speaker: interviewee
text: You know, always read-- you know how they are.

speaker: interviewer
text: Well, if they do come in, at least they'll come in quietly. [LAUGHS]

speaker: interviewee
text: They will read it, you know.

speaker: interviewer
text: So Jay, you want to read the slate?

speaker: interviewer
text: Oh, but-- right. Do you want to mic me up, or--

speaker: interviewer
text: No, I got you, Jay. I have-- I have a camera mic right here, so it's--

speaker: interviewer
text: OK, because I'm going to lean forward, is that OK?

speaker: interviewer
text: Yeah, that's fine. Go ahead.

speaker: interviewer
text: OK. Today is February 12, 1995. The survivor is Yvette Lennon. The interviewer is Jay Straus. The city is Plainview, New York. The country is the United States. And the language is English.

speaker: interviewer
text: OK. I'm ready.

speaker: interviewer
text: OK. How will I know when you begin taping?

speaker: interviewer
text: I'm-- I'll say speed.

speaker: interviewer
text: You'll say speed?

speaker: interviewer
text: Yeah, speed. So that's-- I'm ready. Speed.

speaker: interviewer
text: OK.

speaker: interviewer
text: Go ahead.

speaker: interviewer
text: OK. Could you state your name and spell it?

speaker: interviewee
text: My name is Yvette Lennon. Yvette is Y-V-E-T-T-E. And Lennon is L-E-N-N-O-N.

speaker: interviewer
text: And could you state your name at birth and spell it?

speaker: interviewee
text: What do you mean at birth?

speaker: interviewer
text: Wait. Was that your born-- were you not born with the name Yvette Lennon-- your maiden name?

speaker: interviewee
text: Oh yes, I was born with the name Bonita. So my-- because I had been sick, and I had diphtheria, and usually-- I don't know about the other Jews-- the Sephardic Jews, they change their name when somebody may die, you know. And that's what the name. They gave me this one when I was six years old, so. Now my name is Yvette Lennon.

speaker: interviewer
text: And what was your maiden name?

speaker: interviewee
text: Oh, Assael-- A-S-S-A-E-L.

speaker: interviewer
text: And where were you born?

speaker: interviewee
text: Salonika, Greece.

speaker: interviewer
text: Do you remember the address of the house?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yes. 62 King-- King George Street.

speaker: interviewer
text: And when were you born?

speaker: interviewee
text: I was born-- you mean-- I was-- I guess 1926. I don't know exactly the date. I don't have the date--

speaker: interviewer
text: Right.

speaker: interviewee
text: --because, you know, I don't know, you know.

speaker: interviewer
text: Could we talk a little bit about the pre-war life? What life was like in Salonika?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yeah. Life was very nice in Salonika. It was-- for the Jewish people. Because the Jews lived in Salonika before the Turks. And the-- the commerce was doing very well. They have, you know, they were in contact with Europe, with all these countries. And they had-- they were doing a good business, you know. They had the ports. And that was bringing business to-- to all the places. And actually, Salonika belonged to them. And then the-- also, the Turks were there. But then the Greeks took over in 1921. The Greeks and the-- the Jews were there. But then the Greeks also had their own things to do. And they-- there were about 50,000 Jews in Salonika. And life was very nice, like they tell me, you know, before. Because I was very close to when the war started afterwards-- and that. What I know about when my family said that it was beautiful. They have a good time. And it's a beautiful town, Salonika. Especially-- we lived in-- in a good street. King George Street was when the king used to come. And we could see him passing, because there was a statue of the king-- not much-- just a few miles-- a couple of miles away. And Salonika-- also, our house faced-- faced the ocean. Our backyard was-- right immediately, the ocean came after the backyard. That's why I have those pictures there that we've taken. And so the Greek Jews actually send their children to foreign schools all the time. They didn't send them to Greek schools. They send them-- either there was the Italian school, the French school, the German, the-- the American. And-- and there were a couple of-- about three kinds of French schools. They-- all the teachers say, if it was the French school, they all came from France for the-- for the winter to teach. And then they will go back to their countries. And so each-- every Jewish family that had the German schools-- some people, my sister's husband went to a German school. Because there was no problems then. And that-- that's where they like to go.

speaker: interviewee
text: So I-- my family sent us to French school. So my sister was the first one, you know, to go to the school. She was the oldest, and then my brothers. But my sister-- when I started to go to the French school, my sister had finished the French school. She was finished with the school already, because-- How much older was your sister?

speaker: interviewee
text: 15 Years older. So she-- she already was home. And she was teaching. And my brother had two more years to go. And I had just started. And it was for boys and girls. It was called the [FRENCH]. And-- but they also had a school for just girls. And the problem with me was it that every time I had-- I had to do something and learn something, I had to get out. Because the war started. For instance, when I went to the French school, I was there about two years. And then the order came that-- that the Greek children-- I mean Jewish-- whether they were Jewish, but born in Greece-- they had to go at first to the Greek school and then go after that for the-- you know-- for the high school, they should go to the French school-- whatever school they wanted to. So-- so I had to get out. And I didn't know the Greek language. Because--

speaker: interviewer
text: What language did had you spoken?

speaker: interviewee
text: Spanish, because my parents didn't speak. Because there was the Turks there before. They didn't know Greek.

speaker: interviewee
text: So they taught me about two months. You learn fast, when you are a kid-- to speak. And then I-- I did two years there. And I was already in sixth grade. So I didn't have to stay after sixth grade if I didn't want to. So I went back to the French school. But the law came that-- that the French-- that the-- the Jewish children in-- in foreign schools, they have to learn both. It means that they had Greek in-- in the afternoon, and mostly French in this-- in the morning.

speaker: interviewee
text: So-- and then I was there another three years. And suddenly, the Italians came over to Greece. You know, the war started.

speaker: interviewer
text: Right.

speaker: interviewee
text: And the-- the Nazis sent the Italians here to Greece. Because it's not a big country. So they thought they will win the war. But they didn't.

speaker: interviewer
text: Can you tell me a little bit-- before we get into the war.

speaker: interviewee
text: Yeah.

speaker: interviewer
text: Can we-- can you tell me a little bit about your parents?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yeah. Yeah, my parents-- well, my father, before the war, I heard-- but I wasn't born. I mean, at that time, I wasn't born. But my sister and brother, they said he was very well off. He was doing very well. And-- and they had a good life. But after I was born, it seems like he had a partner at his job, and he stole all his money. And he went to Athens. And he left him broke, you know. And my sister-- is when she started to teach the piano so-- so that we can survive. And she didn't want my father to work anymore, because she was feeling sorry for him. And he was getting older. So-- but life was normal. And we were having-- you know, we didn't-- we had enough to eat, and all this stuff. But-- and it was quiet, peaceful. And we had all the Jews. Sephardic Jews were there. We always met with each other.

speaker: interviewer
text: Was it a religious community?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yeah. Yeah. They did. They had-- we had the synagogues and the temples-- everything. And they-- especially my father was a very religious man. My father would go every day to the temple and every morning, and then especially on a Saturday-- you know, the Sabbath? And he would take me with him, because I was the little one. And I got used to see him that-- I saw that he was a good man. He was such-- you know, like he really loved God, with all his heart.

speaker: interviewer
text: And in the meantime, since the French school closed-- oh, you want to know more about the Jews in Salonika? Sure.

speaker: interviewee
text: The life? Well, I had-- my mother had a brother. I had two brothers. And-- and also she had two sisters and her five. There were five children. My father had only one brother. And he was in France. He left Greece, and he went to live in France. And they had eight children. But my father-- we never saw them since they went to France. But they-- I have my first cousin in France, and she's so wonderful. She's a very nice-- she came here to New York. And she keeps telling me to go to France, but, you know, I always say no. But they were very nice people.

speaker: interviewee
text: That's all he had, the two brothers. So I don't know if you want to know this story. It's my father and my mother were step brother and sister, which I didn't know. And you-- do you want to know this story? Sure.

speaker: interviewee
text: Yeah? So when I was-- I was very young, and my brother was-- my father was telling my brother off, telling him that, when I was your age, I used to work. And, like, he was 15, whatever. So my-- [LAUGHS]

speaker: interviewer
text: --so then he says to my mother, isn't that true? And I say, how can she know? Because you were only 15 years old, how did you know my mother? She says, because she's my sister. [LAUGHS]

speaker: interviewee
text: I say, my mother is your sister? So he says, well, a 1/2-- she's a step-sister.

speaker: interviewer
text: So they were living-- see, my mother lost her father at a very young age. And my father lost his mother at a very young-- no, my father lost-- lost his father, and my mother lost her mother. And then they married, these two people. And they brought all their children in, you know, with them.

speaker: interviewee
text: And they all were, you know, step brother and sister. But my father married my mother as a step brother, you know. There's nothing wrong with that.

speaker: interviewer
text: Right.

speaker: interviewee
text: So anyway. So-- so-- and-- and then, we were so sorry to know that the other people-- my father's brother, you know, my-- my uncle and that, they all left-- left for France. But this is what they wanted to do to make their lives.

speaker: interviewee
text: So I don't know much to tell you more about Greece, like because when I went to school, I mean, I was only 15 years old when I had to leave the camp. I mean, I didn't know much of the story before what the Jews did, and that. But I knew that they did very well-- that they were-- they were prosperous. They never had problems.

speaker: interviewee
text: And when the Greeks came in, there wasn't that much anti-Semitism. You know, they-- they were-- they were getting along well with the Jews. And so, that's all I can tell you about the past-- so until-- we-- until the Nazis came in, you know. And what I wanted to tell you is that-- that the Greeks, as maybe you already know, that the Italians lost the war-- I mean, with the Greeks. They-- they were getting-- see-- see they-- they're not good warriors, the Italians. So they fought the Greece-- the Greeks, and they are-- they pushed them out. But in the meantime, things happen. For instance, my-- my first cousin, he lost his two legs on that war. And that was just a little before we went to the concentration camp. But when Germany saw that the Greeks were winning the war, they got in. And I read that they wasn't going to come to Greece. They were not going to come to Greece. But because this happened, they came.

speaker: interviewer
text: Was there-- was there a preparation for-- for the Nazis coming in? People didn't expect it at all?

speaker: interviewee
text: They didn't expect, because we were so much-- so much far from Germany. We didn't-- they didn't know much what was going on, even though they had started already. There in Greece, we didn't we were not that aware. We knew something was going on.

speaker: interviewee
text: And suddenly, we find out that they have to come here. So even then we didn't know what's all about. What do they want? So what I read in the newspapers that things were not doing very well. But we didn't know until we had to see it for ourselves. And when they sorted the-- the Italians lost the war, immediately, Germany walked in. And-- within three days, they were in. And-- This is while you were in school, this happened?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yeah, while I was in school. And in the meantime, they-- the school had to close, because France already had started the-- Germany started the war with France.

speaker: interviewer
text: Were you living at the school or were you living at home?

speaker: interviewee
text: No, at home.

speaker: interviewer
text: And you would go to the school.

speaker: interviewee
text: Yeah. But since Germany started the war-- the war against France, they had to close the French school. And all the teachers has to leave. So I was out of school, and there was nothing much they can do. I mean, then I was too young to stay at home. But-- so there was the American school that was far away. But I asked my mother. I wanted to learn that language. I wanted. So she sent me there. I was so happy. I loved the American school. It was so easy-- much easier than the French school. I learned the English language. And within-- they-- they had a very good way of teaching there, that in three months, you can talk any language you like. But after three months, you're supposed to learn to speak just English. And you know how girls like to talk. So there was a punishment. If they heard somebody-- heard us saying in not-- talk in another language, they give you that pin. Without nobody knowing, you just put it here, and try to give it to someone else who is going to talk-- [LAUGHS]

speaker: interviewer
text: --another language. Like, you're looking, who is there talking? So-- and then, at the end of the day, they say, who has the pin? And then you get the punishment. So they were trying very hard to just speak English.

speaker: interviewee
text: And-- and by the end of the year, we all spoke-- we all spoke English. And-- so when we went-- in the next year when I went back, we were two months in the American school. And it was about-- oh, it-- this school's office opened, of course, in September. But in February, the principal came and say, girls, you have got to go home. They says, because the-- the England-- I mean Germany started the war with-- with-- with Britain and, you know, the Americans, too.

speaker: interviewer
text: So they-- they came here right away-- I mean-- I mean in Greece right away. And within three days-- and everything changed. That was the beginning for the Jews-- for the beginning of the end. But we still were not aware, because they-- they didn't bother us for a year. They-- they didn't know who was Jewish. The Germans didn't know?

speaker: interviewee
text: No. And we thought, oh, they're-- we're-- they're not doing anything. We didn't know what went on in Europe. So one day--

speaker: interviewer
text: Were there rumors? Or you had no idea?

speaker: interviewee
text: Well, as far as I know, even my family didn't know what's gonna happen. They didn't know. We heard that there's a war. But we didn't know what they're gonna do. That we didn't-- we didn't know they were such cruel people like that. We never even dream of it.

speaker: interviewer
text: So when they came-- when they came to Greece, we were very-- we were doing normal-- we having normal life. And since we were all musicians, even me-- oh-- I have to go a bit backwards. See-- Fine

speaker: interviewee
text: --when we were in Greece, my father, my-- my sister, and brother were very good pianists. They already had started since they were very young. And especially my brother had special talent.

speaker: interviewer
text: So they went to the music school in Greece. And the teacher was one very famous teacher. Although he was Greek, but he grew up in-- you know, where Mozart was born-- Salsburg, I think it is. He grew up in Salsburg.

speaker: interviewee
text: He grew up there, and he was a-- a prodigy. And he became a teacher. Then he came to Greece to teach Greek children. And so they were having lessons, piano lessons, and they played very well, the both of them. Then-- then they started me. My sister started to teach me the piano. But she-- she was very strict. So she would like hit me on the-- [LAUGHS] --on the knuckle every time I would play the wrong note. And then I was crying. My mother said, you can't teach your own sister and you teach other people.

speaker: interviewee
text: So then my brother taught me. And he was screaming at me, saying, she doesn't understand anything. She's stupid. So-- so I did-- no, I did learn the piano. And I was forced with them.

speaker: interviewee
text: The good thing is that my brother had a band, besides, to make some money. And he played the accordion. And then my mother says, you are going to learn the accordion. And they brought me a teacher from his band for three years. And he was a very good accordion player. That's what saved my life-- is I'm telling you, music. If it wasn't for my mother, or for what we knew because of her, we wouldn't have been here today.

speaker: interviewee
text: So I had three years of accordion, I played. I played classical in the accordion. And in the meantime, my brother got that job. So we needed food. We needed money. Because one loaf of bread when the Nazis came in-- say, suppose it was one drachma, then it was 1,000 drachmas. And there was so little food. So we started to get flour at home. You know, we took flour, and we baked it, whatever. Then it's good that my father had some money from some property that he owned. He used it so that we can eat a bit better. He sold the property?

speaker: interviewee
text: He-- yeah-- part of it. And-- and then we went-- they-- they put me to work, too. So I went to play the accordion with my brother's band there. And the Nazis were there. There were Nazis.

speaker: interviewee
text: And the Greek-- it was a Greek place. They said, if-- if they knew you were-- you were Jewish-- [BACKGROUND NOISE] he says, you-- you don't know what would happen, you know? Because already the Greeks knew what's gonna happen.

speaker: interviewee
text: And-- but nobody knows-- knew we were Jewish. So we got a good, you know, we got a good job. And we-- we were having a little money, you know-- enough to survive. And-- and then a year had passed. And suddenly, we realized that the Nazis, they gave us to wear the yellow stars. They went to the Jewish community, and they-- they took all our names. See, in France, in Europe, they didn't do that. They do-- they did try to find the Jewish peoples, but nobody knew where they were. And some went to the camp, some didn't. But with us, the community gave all our names. They went to take the names from the community.

speaker: interviewer
text: The Jewish community gave them your names?

speaker: interviewee
text: I don't know who gave them the name. But they forced them, probably to give the names. Then they knew where everybody was living, and they decided to have ghettos. And these ghettos were outside-- see, where-- like I lived near the ocean and all this-- we had to move out. We had to go to a district which was a poor district. And they used to call it the number 151. That was the name of that district. And-- and there, we had to share a small little house with someone else and that. They had special ghettos. So many would go here. So many would go there. And then there was, at the end of town, where the train-- where the trains were going, you know, start to go, you know. My aunt lived near there-- the other side of town. Because we lived in the middle. My other-- my uncle and my-- his wife and that lived out up all the way uptown. We lived in the middle.

speaker: interviewer
text: This is in the ghetto?

speaker: interviewee
text: Not yet. While we were still not in--

speaker: interviewer
text: I see.

speaker: interviewee
text: --in the ghetto. And then my-- my other aunt lived all the way uptown-- downtown. So we were like-- like everywhere-- like the middle, the up, and down.

speaker: interviewee
text: So they found-- so all these people had to move out from these places. So-- and all the way downtown, where my aunt lived, there was a-- an asylum for the crazy, you know? And my uncle was the director there. They were having them in this place. There they decided to make a-- a tremendous ghetto.

speaker: interviewee
text: That would be the place where-- it was called Baron Hirsch. It means that there is where we were going to go, bit by bit. Say, for instance, if they took one district at a time to send them to the-- to the trains to go to Germany. And so they would-- or to Poland, rather. They-- they would-- they had about, say, 200 people decide-- they decided to send to this ghetto and leave them there for a month. And then the trains will come. And they'll take them. Then the-- another group of people with them-- this is how they were all gonna to leave.

speaker: interviewer
text: Oh, I see.

speaker: interviewee
text: And-- but--

speaker: interviewer
text: So-- so--

speaker: interviewee
text: --we did not-- huh?

speaker: interviewer
text: --so people stayed in their houses until they went to the ghetto?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yeah. They stay in their-- no. They have-- we had to move in those other ghettos. Remember, that I--

speaker: interviewer
text: And-- I see.

speaker: interviewee
text: --told you, in the middle of the town, where my-- somebody I knew-- my-- my sister's grandmother lived around there. And that's where they put us. We couldn't go in the main streets. We had already that-- the [NON-ENGLISH] they called them-- [NON-ENGLISH].

speaker: interviewer
text: The yellow star, you mean?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yeah. That's what they're called. And so you couldn't walk with that in the main streets and that. We had to go over there, to the other places. And from there, then bit by bit, then, we would go by groups to the Baron Hirsch. And then the trains would be waiting there to go straight to Germany-- to Auschwitz. So-- but it took time here, because-- because there was many Jews there.

speaker: interviewer
text: The-- the first ghetto, the one outside of Baron Hirsch-- what was the name of that one?

speaker: interviewee
text: Which one?

speaker: interviewer
text: The-- not-- in other words, you were staying sort of in a intermediary ghetto before you go to Baron Hirsch.

speaker: interviewee
text: Yeah. It's 151, which--

speaker: interviewer
text: Yeah, it's 100--

speaker: interviewee
text: --we used to call it in Spanish, [SPANISH]. Yeah. This is what-- it was the name of the district.

speaker: interviewer
text: Right.

speaker: interviewee
text: And it was small little houses. They put us-- many people together. So we went-- we stayed with-- my brother had a violinist in the orchestra. And he was 45 years old. He was older, considering that we were very young, you know. He had only one grandmother. He didn't have family. So we went to live with them. And so--

speaker: interviewer
text: Did they live there already? Or just--

speaker: interviewee
text: Yeah, yeah, the grandmother lived, I think there. So we decided to go and live with her. And around there, everybody, there were all the Jewish people who had all the little houses that they were there. But we couldn't go away from there.

speaker: interviewer
text: Were there walls around the area?

speaker: interviewee
text: No. No. There was no separation. But if the-- if-- since you have this on you, you know, they-- if they see you walking in one of the other streets, you know, they'll punish you-- whatever they do, you know. I don't know.

speaker: interviewer
text: Did anyone resist trying to go there? In other words, you had to leave your house and go to 151. Did anyone try and put up a fight, or did anyone complain?

speaker: interviewee
text: No, no.

speaker: interviewer
text: They just came in and forced-- the troops-- or how did they make you move?

speaker: interviewee
text: Oh, the troops? No. They told us-- I don't remember very clearly how we got there. I mean, we decided that we go to this house, or to the other house. But that was the district that we supposed to move, and leave all our stuff-- for instance, our piano, everything we had to leave. They took the-- the Greeks, whoever went in the house, they took all our stuff. Because then, there was a Greek woman, that-- she was coming. She was the photographer's wife. Because we had in our backyard a photographer. Because it was a tremendous backyard. And we thought that, I mean, some were-- some anti-Semitics and so. And we don't know who was anti-Semitic. There were good Greek-- Greek people too.

speaker: interviewee
text: But when she saw that we were going to leave, all she wanted to know if she could get what we have. And we-- it hurt us. Can I have this? Can I have that? And my father was so angry. He says, I'm going to break everything. And I'm not going to give everything. She said, my, this taste of feeling sorry for us, you know?

speaker: interviewee
text: But not all the Greeks-- and some Greeks were anti-Semitics, they were. And-- but not-- some were really good, you know. But when the Nazis-- when the Nazis came in, they didn't know who were the Jewish people. See, like, they say they can-- they can spot a Jewish person. I say, they could not spot, because they think that Jewish have a special look or something. They didn't know what they were. When I was in-- in the [NON-ENGLISH], when I was playing, and a German soldier told the waiter, send this to the little girl an apple or something-- that I was sitting with my-- there at the table sometimes, if I'm not playing. And-- [LAUGHS] --the Greek waiter said, there is an apple for Yvette. If he only he knew he was Jewish, he'd throw it in your head. [LAUGHTER]

speaker: interviewer
text: You know? They think like this. They didn't know we were Jewish. So that proves you that there was-- Jews are not a special look that they have, that they-- they pretended we are horrible looking people-- something like that. So there were so many beautiful Jewish girls who came to the camp. And so-- so that was it. So we now were living with-- my sister had-- had met a young man. And he asked to marry her. And he was from the German school. He spoke German beautifully. So he was talking to the Germans.

speaker: interviewee
text: He-- he was doing his own business-- working. He didn't know that this is gonna happen. He was doing business with a German soldier while we were there. And he was saying, you better get out of here. He says, you better get out of here. Go to the mountains. It's not going to be good. I see your families. Your-- your sister-- I mean, your fiance's family, they're such a good people. He says, and I don't want them something to happen to them.

speaker: interviewee
text: See? When they start to see us, that we were people, you know, they were feeling sorry-- some of them. So, but he went away. He says, my sister didn't want to go. He says, take your daughters away from here!

speaker: interviewee
text: We didn't know where to go. They didn't know. They already told us to do those things, but we didn't know. Some went to the mountains. My father-- we are Sephardic Jews, so we could have make a-- we could go to Spain and take certificates. You could do that, but we didn't do it. We didn't know what to do.

speaker: interviewer
text: You didn't know you could do it?

speaker: interviewee
text: They-- no, we didn't know we could do it. My other friend that I had-- I had one friend who was-- she really had the Spanish certificate, although she was Sephardic Jew. She-- she wasn't from Spain. But she-- she stayed in Greece. They didn't touch her, because she had the certificate, you see?

speaker: interviewer
text: The whole time they left her alone?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yeah, they left in Greece. The very end-- the very end, they brought them to Belsen-- you know, to Belsen. But no, they didn't harm them. They just put them there, and then they send them back. They didn't want-- they didn't die there. They came to Belsen right across from us. We saw them. So-- and then came the moment when-- it-- so my sister, we decided they would get married. And we got-- they got married in the backyard with a few other girls. They thought, even if you don't-- you-- you know each other, but just get married, because this is better to go there when you're married. That's what they said. So when my sister got married, and in the back--

speaker: interviewer
text: This is while you were in the ghetto?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yeah, in the ghetto.

speaker: interviewer
text: The 151.

speaker: interviewee
text: In the backyard with a few other people. [BACKGROUND NOISE]

speaker: interviewer
text: We have to change tapes.

speaker: interviewer
text: OK.
